title: Galette de sarrasin
subtitle: Buckwheat crêpes with ham, egg, and Gruyère
excerpt: Traditional Breton buckwheat crêpes, thicker and more substantial than wheat flour ones, with a slightly nutty and earthy flavour. Filled with the classic combination of ham, a fried egg, and melted Gruyère (the complète) and folded around the sides so the egg is exposed in the centre.
sourceNotes: Galettes de sarrasin are documented in Breton cookery literature from at least the eighteenth century. Buckwheat (sarrasin in French, blé noir in Breton dialect) arrived in Brittany from Central Asia in the fifteenth century and became a staple. The crêperie tradition and the complète filling are standard throughout contemporary Brittany.

content.text: Galettes de sarrasin are Breton buckwheat crêpes: thicker and more robust than wheat ones, with a nutty, slightly earthy flavour. Filled with ham, a fried egg, and melted Gruyère (the classic combination, called the complète), then folded at the edges so the egg yolk shows in the centre. The batter rests for at least an hour before cooking.

Buckwheat has no gluten, which makes the crêpes more fragile than wheat ones but also crisper at the edges. The key is heat: a galette cooked over too-low heat comes out pale and floppy. It needs a hot pan to get lacy, crisped edges in about 2 minutes. The first galette almost always needs adjusting. Use it to calibrate the pan temperature and batter thickness before cooking the rest.

items.name: Back bacon
items.prepNote: ham or good cooked ham, thinly sliced

content.text: Whisk the {{buckwheat-flour}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} together in a large bowl. Make a well in the centre and add the batter {{eggs}}. Whisk the eggs into the flour from the centre outward.
Gradually add the {{whole-milk}} and {{water}}, whisking constantly until the batter is smooth and the texture of single cream. Stir in the melted {{unsalted-butter}}.
Cover and refrigerate for at least 1 hour, or overnight.
Heat a 26 cm frying pan or crêpe pan over medium-high heat. Rub the surface with a little butter.
Pour in about 80 ml of batter and swirl straight away to spread it thinly. Cook for 1 to 2 minutes until the edges look dry and lightly crisped.
Scatter a small amount of {{gruyere}} over one half of the galette and lay a few slices of {{back-bacon}} alongside. Crack a filling {{eggs}} egg directly onto the other half.
Cook until the egg white is just set, about 2 minutes. Fold the four edges of the galette inward around the filling, leaving the yolk exposed in the centre. Slide onto a warm plate.

items.fix: Thin the batter with a tablespoon of cold water; cook until just set but not brittle all the way through. A folded galette should bend without cracking
items.cause: Batter too thick, or galette overcooked and brittle

items.fix: Pour the batter in one motion and swirl the pan immediately before the first contact point sets
items.cause: Pan not hot enough, or batter added too slowly
items.symptom: Batter is spreading unevenly with thick patches

items.fix: Cover the pan with a lid for 1 minute once the egg is in. Steam cooks the white without overcooking the yolk
items.cause: Heat too low, or lid not used
items.symptom: Egg white is still raw when the galette is done

content.text: The galette de sarrasin is the food of Brittany the way pasta is the food of Italy: a staple built from what grows there, turned into something particular and regional. Buckwheat tolerates the wet, acidic soils of the Breton interior where wheat does not. The crêperie is the social institution that grew around it: a place to eat galettes and sweet crêpes, usually both, in a particular order. The complète (ham, egg, and cheese) is the reference filling, the one against which all others are measured.
